1.synthesis: Some men come from Judea to Antioch, teaching the brothers that unless they are circumcised according to the custom of Moses they cannot be saved (Acts 15:1). This produces no small disagreement and debate with Paul and Barnabas. The church appoints them, along with some others, to go up to Jerusalem to the apostles and elders about this question (Acts 15:2). They travel through Phoenicia and Samaria, reporting the conversion of the Gentiles—causing great joy (Acts 15:3). In Jerusalem, some believers from the sect of the Pharisees rise up: 'It is necessary to circumcise them and to order them to keep the law of Moses' (Acts 15:5).
1.historical_context: The Jerusalem Council (c. 49 AD) is the pivotal event in early church history for the question of Gentile inclusion. Paul's account in Galatians 2:1–10 provides a complementary perspective: he went up by revelation and presented his gospel privately to the leaders, receiving their endorsement of his Gentile mission without requiring circumcision. The 'men from Judea' represent a rigorist Jewish-Christian position: the covenant sign of circumcision is mandatory for full membership in the covenant people, even for Gentile converts. This was not a fringe view—it had strong precedent in Jewish tradition and seemed to many Jewish believers the only coherent reading of Torah.
1.literary_structure: The opening section (vv.1–5) establishes the presenting problem and the community's decision to seek authoritative resolution from the Jerusalem leadership. The joy of the Phoenician and Samaritan communities on hearing about Gentile conversions (v.3) contrasts with the Pharisaic challenge in Jerusalem (v.5), setting up the council's dual task: affirm the Gentile mission and define its terms. Acts 15 is structurally the center of the book—it resolves the question raised by Cornelius (ch.10), validated by Peter (ch.11), and extended by Paul (chs.13–14), establishing the terms on which the universal mission will proceed.
1.theological_application: The Jerusalem Council's process models ecclesial deliberation at its best: the presenting problem is articulated, the relevant voices are heard (Peter, Barnabas, Paul, James), scripture is engaged, and a decision is reached that the Spirit confirms. The question at stake—can Gentiles be fully included in the covenant community without becoming Jews?—is the most fundamental question the early church faced, and its resolution in favor of Gentile inclusion without circumcision requirement has shaped Christian identity ever since. The church must continue to bring its most divisive questions to communal discernment, including the full range of voices, and to seek the Spirit's guidance through scripture and experience.
1.christology: The question of circumcision is ultimately a question about whether the work of Christ is sufficient for salvation or requires supplementation by Torah observance. The council's answer—implicitly throughout, explicitly in James's ruling—is that Jesus' death and resurrection accomplish what Torah could not: the full inclusion of all peoples in the covenant community. The circumcision requirement would effectively limit the risen Christ's redemptive work to those who undergo the Jewish marker of covenant entry; the council's decision affirms that the marker has been superseded by faith in the one who is himself the covenant in person.

6.synthesis: The apostles and elders gather to consider the question. Peter rises and recalls the Cornelius episode: God chose him to bring the gospel to the Gentiles, gave them the Holy Spirit 'just as he did to us,' made no distinction between them and us, and cleansed their hearts by faith (Acts 15:7–9). 'Now why are you putting God to the test by placing a yoke on the neck of the disciples that neither our fathers nor we have been able to bear? But we believe that we will be saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they will' (Acts 15:10–11). Barnabas and Paul report the signs and wonders among the Gentiles (Acts 15:12). James summarizes: God visited the Gentiles to take from them a people for his name, and this agrees with the prophets (Acts 15:13–18; citing Amos 9:11–12). His judgment: do not trouble the Gentiles, but write them to abstain from things polluted by idols, from sexual immorality, from what has been strangled, and from blood (Acts 15:19–20).
6.historical_context: Peter's speech rehearses the Cornelius episode as council precedent—the Spirit's action then is the theological warrant for the decision now. His identification of circumcision as a 'yoke' that 'neither our fathers nor we have been able to bear' (v.10) is a remarkable internal Jewish critique: the law cannot function as the condition of salvation because no one has been able to keep it. James's Amos citation (LXX version, v.16–17) reads 'the rest of mankind' where the Hebrew reads 'the remnant of Edom'—the LXX provides the universalist reading that supports the Gentile inclusion. The four abstentions (v.20) correspond to Levitical requirements for Gentile aliens in Israel (Leviticus 17–18), enabling Jewish and Gentile believers to share table fellowship.
6.literary_structure: The council speech sequence (Peter, then Barnabas-Paul, then James) creates a legal structure: the primary witnesses (Peter's experience, Paul-Barnabas's experience) establish the facts; James provides the scriptural interpretation and the ruling. James's authority as the final voice—not Peter's—reflects his role as head of the Jerusalem church by this point. The four abstentions are practical requirements for Jewish-Gentile table fellowship rather than doctrinal conditions of salvation; they are distinguishable from the circumcision requirement that was the presenting problem.
6.theological_application: Peter's formulation 'we will be saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, just as they will' (v.11) is the council's soteriological conclusion: the basis of salvation is the same for Jewish and Gentile believers—grace through faith in Jesus. This is the foundation of Christian universalism: not that all paths lead to the same destination but that the same path (grace through Christ) is available to all peoples. James's prophetic interpretation confirms that the Gentile inclusion is not a deviation from Israel's story but its fulfillment: the rebuilding of David's fallen tent is precisely the creation of the new covenant community that encompasses all nations.
6.christology: James's citation of Amos's promise—'that the remnant of mankind may seek the Lord, and all the Gentiles who are called by my name'—is interpreted as fulfilled in the Gentile mission of Paul and Barnabas. The 'rebuilding of David's tent' (v.16) is the resurrection of Jesus the Son of David, through which the Davidic kingdom is expanded to include all nations. The council's decision rests on the conviction that the risen Christ has accomplished what the prophets anticipated: the ingathering of the nations to the God of Israel through the Davidic heir who is now Lord of all.

22.pericope_label: The Letter to the Gentile Believers
22.synthesis: The apostles, elders, and the whole church choose Judas Barsabbas and Silas to accompany Paul and Barnabas to Antioch with a letter (Acts 15:22). The letter greets the Gentile believers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia and disowns the Judaizers: 'We have heard that some persons from us have troubled you with words, unsettling your minds. We gave them no instructions' (Acts 15:24). The messengers are authorized representatives. The letter's requirement is the four abstentions, carried by the Spirit's decision: 'It has seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us' (Acts 15:28). The Antioch believers rejoice at the encouragement (Acts 15:30–31). Judas and Silas, themselves prophets, encourage and strengthen the brothers. Paul and Barnabas and Silas remain and teach (Acts 15:32–35).
22.historical_context: The council letter is one of the few Acts texts that reads as a preserved early document; its formula 'the Holy Spirit and we' (v.28) is a remarkable early instance of the church's self-understanding as operating under Spirit-guidance. Judas Barsabbas and Silas are significant figures: Silas will become Paul's companion on the Second Missionary Journey (Acts 15:40). The four abstentions (idols, sexual immorality, strangled animals, blood) serve as minimal requirements that enable table fellowship between Jewish and Gentile believers without requiring full Torah observance. The letter's disavowal of the Judaizers ('we gave them no instructions') is the Jerusalem church's official repudiation of the circumcision-as-requirement position.
22.literary_structure: The letter's delivery, reception, and the prophetic ministry of Judas and Silas (vv.30–35) provide narrative closure to the council episode. The community's rejoicing (v.31) is the response of those who have been carrying an intolerable burden—the threat that their Gentile faith was insufficient—and have been told by the highest church authority that their faith in the Lord Jesus is complete. The extended teaching at Antioch (v.35) positions Paul for the Second Missionary Journey that begins at verse 36.
22.theological_application: 'It has seemed good to the Holy Spirit and to us' (Acts 15:28) is the model formula for Spirit-guided communal decision-making: the human deliberative process and the Spirit's guidance are presented as concurrent rather than sequential—the Spirit guides the deliberation rather than bypassing it. The church's ongoing discernment in difficult questions proceeds through this same dual agency: Scripture studied, experience narrated, voices heard, and the Spirit guiding toward consensus that the community can own together. The letter's joy-producing reception demonstrates that Spirit-guided decisions bring freedom, not burden.
22.christology: The council's decision is made 'through the grace of the Lord Jesus' (v.11, Peter) and confirmed by 'the Holy Spirit and us' (v.28)—it is both a christological and pneumatological ruling. The Lord Jesus is the one in whose name Gentiles have been saved; the Spirit is the one who has confirmed that salvation through the Cornelius event and through the First Missionary Journey's signs and wonders. The council's letter is ultimately the risen Christ's authorization, through his Spirit-guided community, for the Gentile mission to proceed without the circumcision requirement that would limit his redemptive work.

36.synthesis: Paul proposes to Barnabas to return and visit the brothers in every city where they proclaimed the word to see how they are (Acts 15:36). Barnabas wants to take John Mark; Paul does not, since Mark had withdrawn from the work at Pamphylia (Acts 15:37–38). There is a sharp disagreement; they separate. Barnabas takes Mark and sails to Cyprus. Paul chooses Silas; they go through Syria and Cilicia strengthening the churches (Acts 15:39–41).
36.historical_context: The Paul-Barnabas split is one of the NT's most honest accounts of conflict between major Christian leaders. Paul's assessment of Mark as unreliable ('had withdrawn from them in Pamphylia,' v.38) reflects a serious view of missionary commitment; Barnabas's advocacy for his cousin (cf. Colossians 4:10) reflects a pastoral commitment to the struggling individual. Paul's later relationship with Mark is restored: he calls Mark 'useful to me for ministry' in 2 Timothy 4:11. The separation produced two mission teams rather than one—an outcome that multiplied missionary reach even from human disagreement.
36.literary_structure: The conflict is presented without resolution or theological explanation: both Paul and Barnabas are right within their own frameworks, and the text does not adjudicate between them. Luke's honesty in recording this painful disagreement among leaders is itself significant—Acts does not present the apostolic church as without conflict. The separation produces two mission movements: Barnabas-Mark to Cyprus (where the journey began) and Paul-Silas through Syria-Cilicia (where the Jerusalem Council's letter will be distributed). Timothy will join in the next scene (16:1–3), beginning the Second Missionary Journey properly.
36.theological_application: The conflict between Paul and Barnabas demonstrates that even Spirit-filled leaders can disagree sharply over legitimate questions of judgment, and that human conflict does not necessarily prevent divine purposes from advancing. The church must neither idealize its leaders (pretending conflicts don't happen) nor use conflict as evidence that leaders are disqualified. The 'sharp disagreement' (paroxysmos, from which 'paroxysm') is an intense emotional event; healthy communities can survive and even be strengthened by resolving such conflicts through honest separation rather than false unity or protracted warfare. The integrity of both Paul and Barnabas is preserved precisely because neither suppressed his conviction to maintain surface harmony. The outcome—two mission teams rather than one—is not the intended result but the providential one: the risen Christ works through the grain of competing goods rather than requiring perfect human harmony as the precondition of his mission's advance. The church should not seek artificial unity that silences legitimate difference but the kind of honest resolution that keeps both parties in service, even from different directions.
36.christology: The Lord's purposes advance through and despite the human conflict: two mission teams are sent where one was planned, doubling the potential reach. The risen Christ's mission is not dependent on perfect human harmony—he works through the limitations, failures, and conflicts of his servants as surely as through their triumphs. Mark's restoration and eventual fruitfulness in ministry (including authoring the second Gospel) demonstrates that the one who restores the failed (cf. Peter after denial) continues to work with those who have been written off by others.
